name: Communication and Interaction
description: Children and young people with speech, language and communication needs (SLCN) have difficulty communicating with others. This may involve difficulty understanding language (receptive), expressing themselves (expressive), or both. This area also includes children with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), who may have particular difficulties with social interaction, social communication, and restricted or repetitive patterns of behaviour. Barriers in this area affect a child's ability to process verbal instructions, formulate oral and written responses, understand non-literal language (idiom, sarcasm, inference), and navigate the social demands of collaborative learning.

name: Cognition and Learning
description: Support for learning difficulties covers a wide range of needs, from moderate learning difficulties (MLD) through severe learning difficulties (SLD) to profound and multiple learning difficulties (PMLD). This area also includes specific learning difficulties (SpLD) such as dyslexia (reading and spelling), dyscalculia (number sense and arithmetic), and dyspraxia (motor coordination affecting learning). Children in this area may have difficulty with working memory, processing speed, sequencing, organisation, or the acquisition and retention of literacy and numeracy skills. Barriers are cognitive rather than motivational: the child is working hard but the task demands exceed their current processing capacity.

name: Social, Emotional and Mental Health
description: Children and young people may experience a wide range of social and emotional difficulties which present themselves in many ways. These may include becoming withdrawn or isolated, or displaying challenging, disruptive or disturbing behaviour. This area encompasses attention deficit disorder (ADD), attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), attachment difficulties, anxiety, depression, and other conditions that affect a child's capacity to regulate attention, sustain effort, manage transitions, tolerate frustration, or engage with tasks that require sustained cognitive effort. These are not behavioural choices but genuine difficulties in executive function, emotional regulation, or both.

name: Sensory and Physical
description: Some children and young people require special educational provision because they have a disability which prevents or hinders them from making use of educational facilities generally provided. This includes children with vision impairment (VI), hearing impairment (HI), multi-sensory impairment (MSI), and physical disability (PD). It also covers sensory processing difficulties where a child may have typical hearing and vision but atypical processing of sensory input — commonly associated with autism but not exclusively. Physical needs may affect fine motor control (handwriting, mouse use, manipulating equipment) or gross motor coordination, and may require adapted equipment, modified seating, or adjusted physical expectations.
